title: Transient dynamics of parametric driving for single-electron image current detection in a Paul trap
authors: Baiyi Yu, Andris Huang, Isabel Sacksteder, Hartmut Haeffner
abstract: Nondestructive detection of single-electron motion is crucial for quantum information processing with electrons trapped in Paul traps. The standard approach in Penning traps is to detect the image current induced on the trap electrodes by the electron's oscillatory motion. However, applying this approach in Paul traps for single electrons is currently hindered by motional frequency fluctuations arising from trap anharmonicities and instabilities in the rf trapping field. In this work, we propose a robust detection scheme exploiting the transient dynamics of parametric driving to overcome these limitations. Distinct from traditional steady-state approaches, our method focuses on the transient regime to break the temporal constraints imposed by steady-state assumptions, thereby enabling fast readout. We show that a controlled ramp of the parametric drive effectively locks the frequency of the electron motion in the transient regime, rendering the signal highly resilient to realistic experimental noise and inherent micromotion. This work paves the way for the experimental realization of nondestructive detection of single-electron motion in Paul traps.
summary: This paper proposes a new method for detecting single electrons in Paul traps by using a ramped parametric drive to lock the electron's frequency during the transient regime. This approach overcomes the limitations of traditional steady-state detection, such as frequency fluctuations and signal broadening. The method enables faster and more robust nondestructive readout, essential for electron-based quantum information processing.
main_problem: The nondestructive detection of single-electron motion in Paul traps is hindered by motional frequency fluctuations caused by trap anharmonicities and RF field instabilities.
main_result: A controlled ramp of the parametric drive strength effectively locks the electron's motional frequency in the transient regime, providing a robust and fast readout method resilient to noise and micromotion.
method: The study uses numerical simulations of 3D electron dynamics, comparing instantaneous versus ramped parametric driving protocols using high-order stochastic differential equation solvers.
model_or_system: A single electron trapped in a three-layer PCB Paul trap, coupled to an RLC resonator for image current detection, modeled with nonlinear oscillator dynamics including 6th-order anharmonic terms.
key_observables: Image current (induced voltage), electron motion amplitude and phase, power spectrum of the resonator voltage, and Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR).
important_parameters: Secular frequency of 2pi x 200 MHz, driving strength epsilon = 0.1, temperature T = 4 K, and a parametric drive frequency of approximately twice the secular frequency.
main_assumptions: The model uses a pseudopotential approximation and neglects motional cross-coupling between different axes.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the trap geometry and phase-space trajectories of the separatrix; Fig 2 compares instantaneous vs. ramped driving in time and frequency domains; Fig 3 demonstrates robustness to frequency detuning; Fig 5 shows the probability density distribution of voltage power density.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the detection problem, describes the PCB trap platform and RLC circuit, presents the nonlinear oscillator model, compares driving protocols (instantaneous vs. ramped), analyzes noise impact and statistical distributions, and concludes with implications for experimental realization.
why_interesting: It provides a novel way to utilize transient dynamics rather than steady-state assumptions to achieve robust frequency-locking, which is highly relevant for high-fidelity readout in trapped-particle quantum technologies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Trap cross-section and detection circuit. The three-layer PCB trap uses rf electrodes (orange) for radial (xy) confinement and dc electrodes (blue, pink and grounded yellow) for axial (z) confinement. Pink electrodes connect to a resonator with a bias-T to extract the single-electron image current. (b) Single-electron phase- space motion. Streamlines map the electron trajectories, with local speed and direction encoded by color and arrows, respectively. The red stars denote stable solutions with damping. The red dashed lines mark the separatrix around center. (c) Phase-space scatter plot of the single-electron motion. Initial phases (red/blue dots) evolve into final clusters...

FIG. 2. (a) Single electron motion along x-direction in the time domain comparing the instantaneous (top) with the ramp protocol (bottom). (b) Single electron motion along x-direction in the frequency domain. The red dashed lines in (a) show the relative driving strength ε. The blue and yellow lines in (a) and (b) correspond to the initial phase of the single electron in x-direction ϕx equal to 0 and π/2, respectively. (c) The frequency-domain amplitude of the single electron motion along x-direction for various ϕx. All top panels are with the relative driving strength ε applied instantly at t = 0 µs, whereas all bottom panels with the relative driving strength ε ramping up from 0 to 0.1...
FIG. 3. The induced voltage on the resonator at a frequency of 200 MHz for various values of ϕx and ωx.

Figure 2(a) shows the x-motion for initial phases ϕx = 0 (blue) and π/2 (yellow), comparing an instantaneous step of ε = 0.1 (top) against a 1.0-µs ramp (bottom). Specif- ically, the system is initialized at temperature T with ri = p

FIG. 4. (a) Resonator voltage power spectrum. The solid and dashed lines represent the mean and standard deviation, respectively. Inset: power spetrum statistics at 200 MHz, displaying the density distri- bution (shaded blue area), the mean and standard deviation (center dot with error bars), and the raw power spectrum values of individual trajectories (small dots). The dotted red line in the inset is the thermal noise floor. (b) SNRs of the voltage power signal of the image current with different detection times.
